doc.title: Pay periodic bond interest
doc.event: On {issueDate}, Ramos Co. issues {face} of {statedRateAnnual} bonds ({paymentsPerYear} interest payments) when the market rate is {marketRateAnnual}. Prepare the entry for the next interest payment.

variants.label: Issued at a discount — expense runs ABOVE the cash coupon

entries.caption: Semiannual interest payment (bonds issued at a discount)

lines.label: CV × market × 6/12
lines.why: Expense follows the real borrowing cost: the carrying value times the MARKET rate for half a year. The market rate priced these bonds; it prices the expense too.
lines.trap: Face × stated rate is the CASH coupon, not the expense. At a discount the true cost runs higher than the coupon.

lines.label: expense − cash
lines.why: The gap between expense and cash shrinks the discount, walking the carrying value UP toward face.
lines.trap: Crediting the discount REDUCES this contra-liability. Debiting it here would walk the carrying value the wrong way.

lines.label: face × stated × 6/12
lines.why: Cash paid is fixed by the bond contract: face × stated rate × time. It never changes, whatever the market did.
lines.trap: Don't recompute cash with the market rate — the coupon is contractual.

computationPaths.narration: Effective interest: expense = prior carrying value × market rate × 6/12. Cash stays face × stated × 6/12; the difference amortizes the discount, so the carrying value climbs toward face and next period's expense climbs with it.

Straight line: amortize the SAME slice of the total discount every period (total discount ÷ number of periods, with the final period plugging any rounding). Expense = cash + that slice, so expense is constant instead of climbing.

variants.label: Issued at a premium — expense runs BELOW the cash coupon

entries.caption: Semiannual interest payment (bonds issued at a premium)

lines.label: CV × market × 6/12
lines.why: Same rule as always: carrying value × market rate × time. At a premium the market rate is LOWER than the coupon, so expense runs below cash.
lines.trap: If your expense exceeds the cash coupon on premium bonds, you've flipped the direction — that's the discount pattern.

lines.label: cash − expense
lines.why: The excess of cash over expense burns off the premium, walking the carrying value DOWN toward face.
lines.trap: The premium is DEBITED here (it's an adjunct account being reduced) — crediting it would grow the liability instead.

lines.label: face × stated × 6/12
lines.why: Contractual coupon: face × stated rate × time.
lines.trap: Cash never uses the market rate.

computationPaths.narration: Effective interest: expense = prior carrying value × market rate × 6/12 — which is LESS than the cash coupon. The difference amortizes the premium, so the carrying value steps down toward face.
Straight line: total premium ÷ periods, the same slice every time (final period plugs rounding). Expense = cash − that slice, constant across periods.

variants.label: Issued at par — expense equals the cash coupon

entries.caption: Semiannual interest payment (bonds issued at par)

lines.label: face × stated × 6/12
lines.why: At par the stated and market rates are equal, so the coupon IS the true cost — expense equals cash and there is nothing to amortize.
lines.trap: No discount or premium line belongs in this entry; adding one is the classic par-bond giveaway.

lines.label: face × stated × 6/12
lines.why: Contractual coupon: face × stated rate × time.
lines.trap: Cash never uses the market rate.

computationPaths.narration: At par there is no discount or premium, so BOTH methods produce the same entry: expense = cash = face × stated × 6/12, every period.

memorize.body: Interest EXPENSE = carrying value × market rate × time. The market rate priced the bond; it prices every period's expense.
CASH is contractual: face × stated rate × time — the same number every period, no matter what the market rate is. The expense moves; the coupon never does.
Discount climbs, premium slides: a discount amortizes the carrying value UP to face (expense > cash); a premium amortizes it DOWN to face (expense < cash).

questions.prompt: The bonds are issued at a discount. Using the effective-interest method, what is interest expense for the FIRST semiannual period?

distractors.feedback: That's the cash coupon (face × stated × 6/12). Expense uses the carrying value and the MARKET rate — at a discount it runs above the coupon by exactly the amortization.

traces.label: Carrying value ↔ amortization schedule ↔ expense formula

build.scaffold: Anchor on the two numbers you can always compute: cash (face × stated × time) and expense (carrying value × market × time). The plug between them is the amortization — its direction tells you discount vs premium.
